text: Zarna Garg was born on February 15, 1975, in Mumbai, India. She is an Indian-American stand-up comedian, screenwriter, and New York Times bestselling author. CNBC has called her "the zany, outspoken voice of the Indian American woman."

Zarna Garg's mother died of jaundice when Zarna was 14 years old. After her mother's death, her father pushed for an arranged marriage, which Zarna refused. She fled to the streets of Mumbai rather than accept the arranged marriage, briefly experiencing homelessness in India before immigrating to the United States at age 16 to live with her sister in Akron, Ohio.
Zarna earned a bachelor's degree in finance from the University of Akron and a Juris Doctor (law degree) from Case Western Reserve University School of Law.
Zarna worked as a litigation attorney in Manhattan, specializing in dog bite cases, before leaving law to become a stay-at-home mother for 16 years.

Zarna Garg is married to Shalabh Garg. They got married in 1998. Zarna famously found him by placing an online ad that said: "Only contact me if you want to get married. (NO FRIENDS!) Kindly include your most recent tax returns and medical records." Shalabh responded, and they have been married ever since.

Shalabh Garg is a Wall Street veteran with over 20 years of experience in finance. He now hosts "Finance Dad," a podcast about money, investing, and financial wisdom for families. Zarna has said: "Every time I'm panicked about money or my career or my life in general, he is my first call." Fans interested in Shalabh's podcast can find him at @shalabh.dad on Instagram.

Zarna portrays Shalabh in her comedy as the classic calm, practical finance guy — always playing the "nice cop," which drives Zarna crazy. She jokes that they go on dates where they do finance together for fun. He is the straight man to her chaos.

Zarna and Shalabh have three children: Zoya (23, their eldest daughter), Brij (20, their son), and Veer (14, their youngest son). Her children frequently appear in her comedy content and on her family podcast.

Zarna's daughter Zoya is 23 and a Stanford graduate. She works closely with Zarna on building her brand and comedy career — they are essentially building a family entertainment empire together. Zoya was featured on NPR's "This American Life" podcast exploring questions about parental involvement in education. She appeared alongside Zarna at a live event about Zarna's memoir at Symphony Space in New York.

Brij is Zarna's 20-year-old son and currently in his second year at Cornell University. In Zarna's comedy, she jokes that he is the handsome prodigy son she desperately wants to become a doctor — even though he doesn't want to. She constantly mentions his 6-pack abs with great pride. It was Brij who first encouraged Zarna to try TikTok and personally uploaded her first three videos.

Veer is Zarna's 14-year-old youngest son and attends Stuyvesant High School in Manhattan. He won the Manhattan Borough Spelling Bee Championship — a moment Zarna is incredibly proud of. Zarna jokes in her comedy that she sometimes "forgets about Veer" because he's the third kid, but she always brags about his spelling bee win.

During the pandemic, Brij suggested Zarna try TikTok — an app she had barely heard of. He uploaded her first three videos himself and even added subtitles. Within one week, her account had over a million views. Her first massive viral hit was a video called "I've Never Said I Love You To My Husband," which resonated with millions because it humorously named a real cultural experience in South Asian families. She gained a million TikTok views within three days of that post.

Zarna stayed home as a stay-at-home mother for 16 years before starting comedy. Her kids encouraged her to try stand-up. She performed her first open mic in 2018 in New York City — in a basement under a Mexican restaurant — and had her official debut in February 2019 at Carolines on Broadway.

Zarna Garg started stand-up comedy at age 44. She frequently shares the message that it is never too late to start something new and pursue your dream. Before finding comedy, she tried 18 different businesses.
In 2021, Zarna Garg won Kevin Hart's comedy competition "Lyft Comics" on Peacock, which helped launch her career to a national audience.
Zarna famously campaigned relentlessly to land the opening spot on Tina Fey and Amy Poehler's comedy tour — and got it. She also worked on a project with Chris Rock early in her career.

Zarna Garg's first comedy special, "One in a Billion," was released on Amazon Prime Video in 2023. The 66-minute special covers her humor about matchmaking, entitled kids, clueless husbands, mean aunties, and tyrannical mothers-in-law. Her rapid-fire delivery and punchlines that come "fast and furious" became her signature style.

Zarna Garg's second comedy special, "Practical People Win," was released on Hulu on July 18, 2025. It was filmed at Bimbo's 365 Club in San Francisco. The special takes aim at husbands, kids, in-laws, and anyone who questions an Indian mom's wisdom — including jokes about her mother-in-law (who lives in India), her daughter at Stanford, her sons, and her husband of 25 years.

Zarna made her acting debut in the 2025 romantic comedy film "A Nice Indian Boy," released in theaters on April 4, 2025. She plays Megha, the Indian mother of the main character. The film also stars Karan Soni and Jonathan Groff and is a heartfelt story about an Indian-American man bringing his white fiancé home to meet his traditional family. The director said Zarna "learned to act for the screen faster than I think anyone has ever done in history." Several of her improvised lines became the film's biggest laughs.

CBS has ordered a multi-camera comedy pilot titled "Zarna," starring Zarna Garg — produced by Mindy Kaling and Kevin Hart. Zarna writes, executive produces, and stars. The show is inspired by her own life: a proud immigrant woman who raised her American-born kids on Indian values, now focusing on her own American dream. Warner Bros. Television is the studio.

Zarna Garg's memoir is called "This American Woman: A One-In-A-Billion Memoir," published in April 2025. It is a New York Times Bestseller. The book tells her full story: escaping an arranged marriage, surviving homelessness in Mumbai, immigrating to Ohio at 16, earning her law degree, practicing as a lawyer, spending 16 years as a stay-at-home mom, trying 18 different businesses, and ultimately becoming a stand-up comedian. It is available on Amazon: https://www.amazon.com/dp/0593975022

Zarna hosts "The Zarna Garg Family Podcast" with her husband Shalabh and their three kids as frequent guests. The show drops every Monday and covers modern family problems, parenting, marriage, money, and Indian-American life — all discussed openly and humorously. Available on iHeart and all major podcast platforms.

Zarna Garg has over 2.2 million combined followers across Instagram and TikTok. Her handles are @zarnagarg on Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube. Fans can follow her for daily content about family life, comedy clips, and updates on shows and projects.

Zarna has appeared on major TV shows including: The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon (January 2024 and April 2025), Late Night with Seth Meyers (twice), Today with Jenna & Friends, The Kelly Clarkson Show, GMA3, and The View.
Zarna Garg lives in New York City with her husband and children.

Zarna's comedy is sharp, opinionated, high-energy, and family-centered. She draws from her life as an Indian-American immigrant mother — jokes about her husband, her kids' very American habits clashing with her very Indian expectations, and the chaos of running a loud desi household in New York. Her humor always comes from a place of love.
Fans can find Zarna's upcoming show dates and buy tickets at https://zarnagarg.com/tickets/
For merch and general information about Zarna Garg, fans can visit https://zarnagarg.com

Zarna writes a weekly 'Monday Motivation' series on social media, sharing raw, no-nonsense business and life advice from her own experience as an immigrant entrepreneur and mother. She has been doing it consistently for over two years. Topics include sales, hustle, building a business from scratch, mindset, and the reality of immigrant ambition in America.

Zarna openly admits she lives a 'terrified life' because she lost everything at age 15 and spent years as what she calls a 'semi-permanent grifter.' Her deep anxiety about money and financial security is a core part of who she is — she wishes she were less worried, but says 'it's not in my destiny.' This fear is also what drives her relentless work ethic.

Zarna's core life philosophy — one she says to her kids and repeats constantly — is 'the long way is the short way.' It means there are no real shortcuts; the slow, unglamorous path of doing the work is always faster in the end than trying to skip steps.

Zarna sees herself as an entrepreneur who happens to do comedy, not just a comedian. She is intensely focused on building a business, generating revenue, and creating a lasting brand. She is blunt about money: 'The only thing that protects you in this crazy world is money.' She believes sales is the single most important skill anyone can learn.

Zarna tried 18 different businesses before finding comedy. She found her calling not by following a dream but by following her curiosity and staying open to failure. She often says: 'The only reason I found my thing is because I kept taking chances and making mistakes until I heard the click in comedy.'

Divya Gugnani is a close friend and business mentor of Zarna. She is a successful female entrepreneur. Her advice to women in business: be adaptable, embrace change as the only constant, and be ruthlessly shameless about going after opportunities.

Zarna's 'green juice' skit is one of her most viral moments: her kid catches her drinking what is clearly a margarita, and Zarna insists it is just 'green juice' with cucumber and jalapeño. When pressed, she admits 'there's a little tequila — it's a garnish.' The joke captures her signature deflection humor.

One of Zarna's signature comedic characters is 'the world's leading almost therapist.' In this recurring bit she plays an unlicensed therapist who gives hilariously wrong — but deeply confident — advice to whoever walks into her 'practice.' Clients include her own children, her husband Shalabh, celebrities like Martha Stewart and Malala, airline pilots, and anyone else who needs fixing. The humor comes from her mixing South Asian aunty wisdom, blunt unsolicited opinions, and total disregard for actual therapeutic technique. She is never wrong. She always has a solution. It usually makes things worse.
